slides.title: How to Study Effectively (Without Studying More Hours)
slides.subtitle: A practical system focused on retrieval, spacing, and deliberate practice
body: Short, repeatable methods to turn study time into durable learning.
Focus: learn smarter, not longer.

slides.title: The Problem
body: Most people study but don't actually learn.
Re-reading notes feels productive but doesn't stick.
Highlighting creates familiarity, not fluency.
Real learning happens when your brain must retrieve and apply information.
slides.note: If studying feels easy, it's probably not working.

slides.title: Core Insight
body: Three rules separate seeing from doing:
1) Active recall: force retrieval.
2) Spaced repetition: revisit over time.
3) Deliberate practice: target mistakes, not comforts.
slides.note: These three are the difference between 'I saw it' and 'I can do it.'

slides.title: Active Recall — What to Do
body: Replace passive methods with retrieval-based tasks.
Blurting: close notes, write everything you remember, then check gaps.
Practice questions: use exam-style problems, not summaries.
Teach-back: explain the concept as if tutoring someone.
Quick example: instead of 'read the chapter', 'answer 10 questions, mark errors, then fix them.'

slides.title: Spacing and Interleaving — How to Schedule
body: Spacing beats cramming: shorter sessions across days are more effective than one long block.
Example schedule: Day 0 learn; Day 1 recall; Day 3 recall; Day 7 mixed practice.
Interleaving: mix topics rather than studying one subject for hours straight.
It will feel harder in the moment — that difficulty is what makes it stick.

slides.title: Feedback Loop — Improve Fast
body: After every session ask: What did I get wrong? Why? What pattern keeps repeating?
Turn mistakes into your study plan: spend most time on weak areas.
Practical rule: allocate roughly 70% of time to weak areas and 30% to review and retention.

slides.title: Simple 45-Min Study Template
body: 0–5 min: set a specific, measurable goal for this session.
5–25 min: active recall via questions or focused practice.
25–35 min: mark answers and maintain an error log.
35–45 min: re-attempt wrong questions and schedule the next review.
slides.note: No fancy motivation needed - just a system.

slides.title: Why This Works vs Re-reading
body: Re-reading creates familiarity without reliable recall or application.
Retrieval forces you to practice recall and exposes gaps to fix.
Spacing prevents rapid forgetting by reactivating memories over time.

slides.title: When to Use This
body: Exam preparation and timed assessments.
Long-term retention of course material.
Practicing problem-solving and application under pressure.

slides.title: How to Start and Scale
body: Start with one 45-minute session per topic to build the habit.
Schedule follow-ups in a calendar or spaced-repetition tool.
Use practice-question banks, flashcards, teach-backs, or study groups to vary retrieval.

slides.title: Evidence & Proof (Placeholders)
body: Placeholder: add supporting studies, references, or summary of results here.
Placeholder: insert before/after metrics, case studies, or sample student outcomes.

slides.title: Closing — One Change to Make
body: Effective studying is about technique, not time.
If you make one change: swap re-reading for retrieval (practice questions + active recall) and then space it out.
